hints: Choose a = max of f on [0,1]; f'(x)=0 at x=2/3 gives a=16/9.
solution: The tightest rectangle uses $a=\max f=f(\tfrac23)=\tfrac{16}{9}$. Each point is accepted with probability $\dfrac{\int_0^1 f}{1\cdot a}=\dfrac{1}{16/9}=\dfrac9{16}$. The number of rejects before an accept is geometric with mean $\dfrac{1-p}{p}=\dfrac{7/16}{9/16}=\dfrac79.$

hints: The density is a Laplace distribution, symmetric about ╬╝.
Symmetric distributions have zero third central moment.
solution: The density $f(x)=\tfrac{\lambda}2 e^{-\lambda|x-\mu|}$ is symmetric about $\mu$, so all odd central moments vanish. Hence the skewness $\mathbb{E}[((X-\mu_X)/\sigma_X)^3]=0.$

hints: Each take must be preceded by a roll, so a 'cycle' = (rolls until Ōēźn) + 1 take.
Cycle length 20/(21ŌłÆn)+1 actions; #cycles=100/(cycle); take value averages (20+n)/2.
solution: Since each take needs a fresh roll, a cycle is (roll until $\ge n$) then take, of length $\tfrac{20}{21-n}+1$ actions, each yielding a take worth $\tfrac{20+n}2$. Expected payout $=\dfrac{100}{\tfrac{20}{21-n}+1}\cdot\dfrac{20+n}{2}$, maximized at $n=6$: $\dfrac{300}{7}\cdot13=\dfrac{3900}{7}.$

hints: There are nŌłÆ5 possible starting positions for a 6-chain; each is increasing with probability 1/6!.
solution: Each of the $n-5$ starting positions gives an increasing $6$-chain with probability $\tfrac1{6!}=\tfrac1{720}$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac{n-5}{720}$. Setting this to $1$ gives $n=725.$

hints: Condition on N: P(no heads | N=n)=(1/2)Ōü┐.
Player 1 wants the numbers far apart; Player 2 wants them close.
From any pure pair, one player can profitably deviate (P1 to an endpoint, P2 toward P1).
solution: Player 1 maximizes $|a-b|$ and Player 2 minimizes it. For any fixed $(a,b)$, Player 2 can move toward $a$ to reduce the distance, or Player 1 can move to an endpoint to increase it ŌĆö so no pair is stable. There are $0$ pure-strategy Nash equilibria.
Here $\bar x=0,\bar y=1$, $S_{xy}=7$, $S_{xx}=10$, so $\hat\beta_1=0.7,\hat\beta_0=1$. The residuals are $0.4,-0.3,0,-0.7,0.6$ with $\text{SSE}=1.10$, so $s^2=\dfrac{1.10}{5-2}=\dfrac{11}{30}.$

hints: Odd and divisible by 3 means ŌēĪ3 (mod 6).
solution: Numbers that are odd and divisible by $3$ are exactly those $\equiv3\pmod6$: $3,9,\dots,2019$. Their count is $\dfrac{2019-3}{6}+1=337.$

hints: Scale to 600 units: Alice 5/min, Bob 6/min. After 40 min they've built 440, leaving 160.
Three together finish 160 in 10 min: (5+6+c)┬Ę10=160 ŌćÆ c=5.
solution: Take the pipe as $600$ units: Alice builds $5$/min, Bob $6$/min. In $40$ min they finish $440$, leaving $160$, done in $10$ more minutes by all three: $(5+6+c)\cdot10=160\Rightarrow c=5$. So Charlie alone needs $600/5=120$ minutes.

hints: Starting 1 make, 1 miss, the made-fraction is a martingale fixed at 1/2 (P├│lya urn).
So the expected fraction made over 100 attempts is 1/2.
solution: This is a P├│lya urn: the running fraction made is a martingale equal to its start $\tfrac12$. Hence the expected number made in $100$ attempts is $100\cdot\tfrac12=50.$

hints: Doubling data: X'=2X so (X'ßĄĆX')Ōü╗┬╣=(1/4)(XßĄĆX)Ōü╗┬╣; but the y's (and errors) also double, so Žā'┬▓=4Žā┬▓.
solution: With $X'=2X$, $(X'^TX')^{-1}=\tfrac14(X^TX)^{-1}$. But doubling the data also doubles the responses and errors, so $\sigma'^2=4\sigma^2$. Hence $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta')=4\sigma^2\cdot\tfrac14(X^TX)^{-1}=\sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1}=\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta)$, giving $c=1.$

hints: Number of hearts is hypergeometric (n=8, K=13, N=52).
solution: The count of hearts is hypergeometric, with $\mathrm{Var}=n\cdot\tfrac{K}{N}\big(1-\tfrac{K}{N}\big)\cdot\tfrac{N-n}{N-1}=8\cdot\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac34\cdot\tfrac{44}{51}=\dfrac{22}{17}.$
